map.subtitle: Big Idea 5: Impact of Computing   •   1 day   •   LO IOC-1.E
map.intro: Everything for this topic lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Day 1 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, share-out cues, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with the QOTD warm-up, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Day 1 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Crowd Project Triage and Exercise 2: The Crowd Consultant (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — The Power (and Price) of the Crowd (3-part: Opening, Mid-lesson, Closing — all inside the single teach day)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 6 questions on LO IOC-1.E, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the instruction you project and teach from (one teach day: problem-solving at scale — open data and collaboration via computing — then classifying crowdsourcing's two famous shapes).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Scheduling: Topic 5.4 is a 1-day topic. The quiz runs as the opener of the NEXT class (spaced retrieval), so the topic never silently consumes a second day. Topic 5.5 (Legal and Ethical Concerns) begins immediately after the quiz.

exercise1.title: Crowd Project Triage
exercise1.intro: You are the intake reviewer for a website that showcases crowd-powered projects. Five submissions are waiting for a ruling before the next feature goes live. For each submission you will read how it works and who participates, then decide whether it is genuinely crowdsourcing, name its model, identify what the crowd contributes, and explain why scale matters. Part A works the submission log; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objective IOC-1.E.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: Submission Log: Five Projects Awaiting Review
exercise1.narrative: The site's editor exported the five pending submissions with notes from each organizer. Your job is to triage them. Read the log row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: test the definition first (a large number of people? via the Internet?), name the model and the contribution, then state why the project needs a crowd at all.

rows: SkyCount (bird-sighting app)
Anyone photographs birds; the app timestamps and uploads each sighting to a shared research dataset
Hundreds of thousands of volunteers worldwide, almost none of them biologists
One observation at a time, captured on their own phone
FundTheField (community campaign)
A youth robotics team posts its budget online; supporters pledge small amounts until the goal is met
About 3,000 backers, most giving under $20
A small pledge of money
TapTraffic (navigation app)
Drivers' phones automatically share speed and location, which the app merges into a live congestion map
Millions of drivers, contributing passively while they drive
A stream of real-time movement data
ExpertDesk (support portal)
A software company routes customer tickets to its twelve in-house engineers, who answer within a day
Twelve salaried engineers at one company
Professional troubleshooting labor
PhraseForge (archive project)
A history museum posts scanned letters online; volunteers transcribe a page whenever they have a spare minute
Tens of thousands of registered volunteers, no history training required
One transcribed page at a time

exercise1.scenarioNote: Submissions adapted from the Day 1 bell ringer and the classify-five-projects worked table; terminology and EKs follow the College Board AP Computer Science Principles Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2023).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: which one submission does NOT belong on a crowd-powered showcase, and what single detail in its log row disqualifies it?

partA.q: Submission 4 (ExpertDesk) must be rejected from the showcase. Using the CED definition of crowdsourcing, name BOTH ingredients the definition requires and show which one(s) ExpertDesk fails — even though its twelve engineers are highly skilled.
partA.answer: Crowdsourcing is the practice of obtaining input or information from a LARGE NUMBER of people VIA THE INTERNET (EK IOC-1.E.4) — the two ingredients are scale and the Internet channel. ExpertDesk fails the scale ingredient: twelve salaried engineers at one company are a small hired team, not a large number of people, and participation is closed (employment), not open contribution. Skill is irrelevant to the ruling — the definition never mentions expertise. This is ordinary workplace collaboration (Topic 1.1), not crowdsourcing. Full credit requires naming both ingredients and rejecting on scale/openness, not on quality.

partA.q: Submission 1 (SkyCount) is citizen science. Point to the THREE parts of the CED's citizen-science definition in SkyCount's log row, then explain why the research needs hundreds of thousands of contributors instead of a university field team.
partA.answer: EK IOC-1.E.3's three parts, all visible in the row: (1) distributed individuals — hundreds of thousands of volunteers worldwide; (2) many may not be scientists — 'almost none of them biologists'; (3) they contribute relevant data to research using their own computing devices — each observation is captured and uploaded on the volunteer's own phone. Scale matters because coverage is the product: a field team can stand in a handful of places on a handful of days, while a distributed crowd observes everywhere, continuously — which is how distributed and citizen science have genuinely affected how scientific problems get solved (EK IOC-1.E.2).

partA.q: Submission 2 (FundTheField) collects money, not data. Explain why it still counts as crowdsourcing, name the collaboration model it demonstrates, and state why 3,000 backers succeed where the team's three earlier grant applications failed.
partA.answer: It satisfies the definition: pledges are INPUT obtained from a large number of people via the Internet (EK IOC-1.E.4) — the definition covers input or information of any kind, not just data. The model is EK IOC-1.E.6's new collaboration model: crowdsourcing connecting a social cause (a youth robotics team) with funding. The scale logic: a grant needs one large yes from one gatekeeper, while crowdfunding aggregates 3,000 small yeses — no single backer risks much, but together they clear the goal, and the Internet is what makes gathering 3,000 tiny contributions cheap enough to work.

partA.q: Submissions 3 (TapTraffic) and 5 (PhraseForge) collect completely different things — passive movement data versus deliberate transcription work. Classify each one's model and contribution, then explain what EK IOC-1.E.5 says both projects prove about collaboration via computing.
partA.answer: TapTraffic is citizen-science-style data contribution: millions of drivers supply real-time movement data from their own devices, passively — no effort required, but still input from a large number of people via the Internet (EK IOC-1.E.4). PhraseForge is the input/information model: volunteers contribute knowledge work (transcribed pages), one small task at a time. Both prove EK IOC-1.E.5 — human capabilities can be enhanced by collaboration via computing: no individual could map a city's live traffic or transcribe a museum's entire archive, but computing coordinates millions of tiny contributions into exactly those outcomes.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: The city where SkyCount is based releases its air-quality sensor records as free public data. Describe one problem the public could IDENTIFY, one solution someone could DEVELOP, and one way results could be DISSEMINATED — and name the EK principle that makes all three easier.
partB.answer: Per EK IOC-1.E.1, widespread access to information and public data facilitates the identification of problems, development of solutions, and dissemination of results. Identify: residents comparing neighborhood readings could spot a pollution hotspot near a school. Develop: a developer could build an app that routes walkers and joggers around the worst blocks, or a map overlaying readings with traffic. Disseminate: the findings could be published on neighborhood sites, social feeds, or a local news story reaching people who never opened the dataset. Any reasonable trio earns credit; each answer must match its verb.

partB.q: A skeptic says: 'These crowd projects don't enhance anything — people could always count birds and donate money.' Use one submission from the log to explain what collaboration VIA COMPUTING adds that was impossible before.
partB.answer: The activities existed; the SCALE and coordination did not — EK IOC-1.E.5 says human capabilities can be enhanced by collaboration via computing. Take SkyCount: people have always watched birds, but without computing there was no way to collect, timestamp, validate, and pool hundreds of thousands of observations from around the world into one analyzable dataset in real time. The enhancement is the aggregation: computing turns isolated individual acts into a coordinated instrument with continental coverage. (FundTheField works too: passing a hat was always possible, but reaching 3,000 willing strangers cheaply was not.)

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: SkyCount's dataset feeds real published research, yet anyone — including careless or dishonest users — can upload sightings. Describe two quality-control mechanisms a citizen-science platform can use to keep bad data out WITHOUT requiring contributors to be experts, and explain why that requirement would defeat the whole model.
partB.answer: Mechanisms (any two): automated plausibility checks (is this species realistic for that date and location?), flagging unusual reports for human/regional reviewers, requiring photo evidence that others can verify, cross-checking against overlapping reports from independent contributors, and reputation systems that weight trusted contributors' records more heavily. Requiring expert contributors would defeat the model because crowdsourcing's power is scale (EK IOC-1.E.4) and citizen science explicitly runs on people who may not be scientists (EK IOC-1.E.3) — filter the crowd down to credentialed experts and you are back to a small team with small coverage. Validate the DATA, not the people.

exercise2.title: The Crowd Consultant
exercise2.subtitle: 4 clients - classify and justify
exercise2.intro: You are the consultant four clients call when they want 'one of those crowd things.' For each scenario, test the CED definition (a large number of people? via the Internet?), rule crowdsourcing or not, name the model and what the crowd contributes, and explain why scale matters. Advanced students also state what the crowd accomplishes that a small hired team never could. Vague 'just put it online' advice scores zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): the definition test (large numbers + the Internet) + the model + what the crowd contributes, for all 4 scenarios. Enrichment (optional): also explain why the project succeeds only at scale (EK IOC-1.E.5).
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every scenario
method: Anchor your gut ruling before analyzing. Writing your own answer first keeps a convincing wrong option from swaying you.

Two ingredients, both required: input or information from a LARGE NUMBER of people, obtained VIA THE INTERNET. A small or closed group fails, no matter how skilled. Expertise is never one of the ingredients.
Step 3 - Name the model and the contribution

If it passes, say WHAT the crowd hands over: data on their own devices (citizen science), money (funding models), or other input like edits and reviews. A ruling without the contribution named is incomplete.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (the scale test): strong consultants also state why the crowd is irreplaceable — what the project gets from thousands of contributors that no hired team could deliver (coverage everywhere at once, continuous observation, thousands of small risks instead of one big one). If the project would work just as well with 20 handpicked people, it is collaboration, not crowdsourcing — and your report should say so.
fields: Definition test (a large number of people? via the Internet?) + your ruling:
Model + what the crowd contributes (data, money, or other input):
Enrichment — why only a crowd can do this (what scale buys):

scenarios.text: A seismology lab wants earlier earthquake warnings. It releases a free app that quietly uses each phone's motion sensor: when thousands of phones in one area shake at the same moment, the lab's servers detect the quake seconds before it spreads. Two hundred thousand people have installed the app. Rule on the project.

key.signs: Passes both ingredients: 200,000 installed phones is a large number of people, and the sensor readings travel to the lab via the Internet. The contributors do nothing deliberate and know no seismology — irrelevant on both counts, since the definition (EK IOC-1.E.4) requires scale and the channel, never effort or expertise.
key.mechanism: Ruling: crowdsourcing — specifically citizen science. Distributed individuals, almost none of them scientists, contribute relevant data (motion readings) to research using their own computing devices (EK IOC-1.E.3), and science is genuinely affected by the distributed approach (EK IOC-1.E.2). The contribution is passive sensor data; the phones are the instruments.
key.subtype: Scale is the entire detection mechanism: one shaking phone means a dropped phone, but thousands shaking in the same second means an earthquake. No lab could plant 200,000 sensors across the region — the crowd IS the sensor network, which is collaboration via computing enhancing human capability (EK IOC-1.E.5).

scenarios.text: A rural clinic needs a solar-powered vaccine refrigerator that no local funder will pay for. A nurse posts the project on a platform where anyone can pledge money; 4,800 strangers pledge an average of $9 each, and the refrigerator ships within a month. Rule on the project.

key.signs: Passes both ingredients: 4,800 contributors is a large number of people, and the platform gathers their pledges via the Internet. The input here is money rather than data — the definition covers input or information of any kind (EK IOC-1.E.4).
key.mechanism: Ruling: crowdsourcing — the funding model. This is EK IOC-1.E.6 almost verbatim: crowdsourcing offering a new model for collaboration by connecting a social cause with funding. The crowd contributes many small pledges; the platform aggregates them into one purchase no single contributor would or could make alone.
key.subtype: Scale converts risk: one funder must bet big and say one expensive yes, while 4,800 strangers each risk the price of lunch. The Internet makes collecting thousands of tiny contributions nearly free, which is why this collaboration model simply did not exist before it (EK IOC-1.E.5, E.6).

scenarios.text: A television network announces its cooking competition will be 'judged by the crowd': five celebrity chefs, selected by producers, will score each dish through a private scoring website that only the five of them can access. Rule on the project.

key.signs: Fails the definition's first ingredient: five handpicked judges are not a large number of people, and access is closed — participation by producer invitation is the opposite of open contribution. Using a website does not rescue it; the Internet channel alone is only one of the two required ingredients (EK IOC-1.E.4).
key.mechanism: Ruling: NOT crowdsourcing — a small, selected expert panel using an online tool. Marketing language ('judged by the crowd') is not the test; the definition is. If the network wanted actual crowdsourced judging, it would open scoring to its large viewing audience via the Internet — input from millions, expertise optional.
key.subtype: The scale test exposes it instantly: this project works identically with the same five people on paper ballots, so the crowd adds nothing — there is no crowd. Contrast with a real audience vote, where the result only means something at scale. Exam distractors are built exactly like this scenario: expert panels dressed in crowd vocabulary.

scenarios.text: After a hurricane, a relief agency posts fresh satellite images of the flooded region on an open mapping site. Overnight, 11,000 volunteers around the world — students, gamers, retirees — trace the passable roads and mark damaged bridges tile by tile. By morning, rescue drivers are navigating with the volunteer-built map, and the agency publishes it for every other relief group to reuse. Rule on the project.

key.signs: Passes both ingredients overwhelmingly: 11,000 open, self-selected volunteers worldwide contributing via the Internet. The contributors have no cartography credentials — and per EK IOC-1.E.4 and E.3's logic, none are needed; each tile is small enough for anyone to trace.
key.mechanism: Ruling: crowdsourcing — the input/information model, with the whole EK IOC-1.E.1 chain running through it. Open access to the satellite imagery (public data) let volunteers identify the problems (blocked roads), develop the solution (the traced map), and disseminate the results (published for every relief group). The crowd contributes knowledge work: traced roads and marked bridges, tile by tile.
key.subtype: Scale buys speed no organization can hire: 11,000 people working one tile each finish overnight what a mapping team would need months to produce — and hurricane response is measured in hours. This is the exam's showcase of EK IOC-1.E.5: collaboration via computing enhancing human capability precisely when it matters most.

discussion.title: The Power (and Price) of the Crowd
discussion.minutes: ~20 min total - 3 parts - Opening + Mid-lesson + Closing (all Day 1)
discussion.overview: You are already in several crowds without ever signing up: your phone feeds a traffic map, your reviews train a ranking, your photo tags teach software what a face looks like. Across three short windows in this lesson, your group will decide what makes a crowd genuinely powerful, what you would — and would not — hand one, and whether the people who quietly supply a crowd's input are owed anything by the platforms that profit from it.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Trade bell-ringer projects with your group. For each one, apply the shrink test: would it still work with 100 people instead of 10,000? If yes, what would you change so the crowd becomes truly essential?
Question 2. List the crowds you have already joined without meaning to — traffic data, reviews, photo tags, game telemetry. Pick one: what does the crowd get from you, and what do you get back?
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~6 min, after the two-shapes comparison
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The one-question rule
items.text: Classifying any crowd project comes down to one question about its contributors. In one sentence your whole group agrees on, write that question — and state how the answer separates citizen science from funding-style models.

items.label: Task B - What would YOU give a crowd?
items.text: Debate for about 3 minutes: rank what you would contribute to a stranger's project — your money, your location data, your photos, an hour of your labor. What makes some contributions feel cheap and others feel expensive? Record the group's dividing line.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after Common AP Traps
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The flooded-town problem
items.text: A small town floods every spring, and city hall is out of ideas and money. Design a crowd-powered response with at least three elements — for example: releasing the town's rainfall and drainage records as public data, recruiting residents' phones or rain gauges as sensors, and funding repairs through small online pledges. Label every element with its model and what the crowd contributes, and mark the one element that would fail without a large crowd.
Our group's verdict (which element depends most on scale + one-sentence justification):
